name: Template Engine
description: Given SymbolInfo from Phase 1 (function/method/struct/class signatures with names, parameters, return types, receivers), deoxy renders proper doc comments in each target language's format. This phase builds the template engine (Go text/template-based), per-language template definitions (GoDoc prose, Doxygen for C/C++, Google-style Python docstrings, Rustdoc), the configuration system (.deoxy.yaml loading), and the generator orchestrator that wires config → scanner → parser → engine into the final pipeline. No source file modification (Phase 3) — only in-memory rendering with verification tests.
anti_goals: Do NOT insert comments into source files (that is Phase 3 — CLI integration)
Do NOT implement CLI commands (Phase 3 introduces cobra commands: generate, init, watch)
Do NOT build the VS Code extension sidecar (Phase 4)
Do NOT implement smart text / name inference (Phase 5 — getter/setter/ctor detection, name splitting)
Do NOT add AI-generated descriptions (out of scope permanently)
Do NOT handle watch mode or fsnotify (Phase 3)
Do NOT modify the tree-sitter query files from Phase 1 — they are stable and correct
Do NOT add dependencies beyond the Go standard library — text/template is sufficient
roadmap_success_criteria: Python function `def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str` generates Google-style docstring with Args: and Returns: sections

C function `int divide(int dividend, int divisor)` generates `/**
 * @brief ...
 * @param dividend ...
 * @param divisor ...
 * @return ...
 */`
Template tag order is configurable (brief first vs. param first)
Go output uses GoDoc convention (prose, no @param tags) by default, configurable to Doxygen-style via config
Config file (.deoxy.yaml) can set per-language docstyle preferences

tasks.title: Create internal/template/engine.go — Core template engine with Render()
tasks.description: Build the template engine that takes a SymbolInfo struct and a language identifier, applies the language's Go text/template, and returns a formatted doc comment string. The engine provides template helper functions (FuncMap) for brief description generation, parameter/return formatting, and comment-line prefix management. Short descriptions use basic derivation: function name → verb (lowercase first letter + 's' suffix for simple cases, e.g. 'Add' → 'adds'), param descriptions use ordinal templates ('the first operand', 'the second operand'), return descriptions use type-based phrases ('the result', 'the value'). This is intentionally simple — smart text inference comes in Phase 5.

Engine.description: Template engine that renders SymbolInfo into language-specific doc comments using Go text/template. Thread-safe after construction.

fields.name: templates
fields.comment: Compiled templates, keyed by language name ('go', 'python', 'c', 'cpp', 'rust')

fields.name: funcs
fields.comment: Shared helper functions available to all templates

methods: func New(tpls map[string]string) (*Engine, error) — compiles raw template strings into text/template.Template objects; returns error if any template fails to parse

func (e *Engine) Render(info symbol.SymbolInfo, lang string) (string, error) — executes the template for the given language against SymbolInfo data; returns formatted doc comment string or error if language is unknown

brief.behavior: Generates a brief description sentence from the symbol name and signature. For functions: lowercases first letter of name, appends parameter names with 'and' joining, and appends return phrase. E.g., Name='Add', params=[{a,int},{b,int}], returns=['int'] → 'adds a and b and returns the result.' For methods: includes receiver context. For structs/classes: 'represents a ...'
examples: brief('Add', [{a,int},{b,int}], ['int']) → 'adds a and b and returns the result.'
brief('greet', [{name,str},{age,int}], ['str']) → 'greets a person with the given name and age.'

paramDesc.behavior: Generates a description for a parameter at the given ordinal position. For the first param: 'the first operand' / 'the X to Y'. For second: 'the second operand'. Beyond that: 'the Nth parameter'. Also uses param name as hint (e.g., 'name' → 'the name'). Basic mapping, smart text deferred to Phase 5.

returnDesc.behavior: Generates a return description based on return types. Single return: 'the result'. Multiple returns: 'the first return value', 'the second return value'. Return type name is included if short (e.g., 'the int result').

joinParams.behavior: Joins parameter names with ', ' and ' and ' for use in brief sentences.

commentPrefix.behavior: Returns the comment prefix for the language: '//' for Go, '#' for Python, '//' for Rust, ' *' for Doxygen

template_variables.description: Each template receives a data context with the following top-level variables, derived from SymbolInfo fields:

error_handling.unknown_language: Render returns error 'unknown language: X' when lang is not in the templates map
error_handling.template_error: Render wraps template.Execute errors with context (language name, symbol name)
error_handling.empty_symbol: Render handles empty Name gracefully — returns empty string or placeholder comment
error_handling.nil_receiver: Templates check HasReceiver before accessing Receiver fields to avoid nil pointer panics

specification.rationale: Using Go text/template (stdlib) avoids external dependencies. The template + FuncMap pattern is the standard Go approach (same as Helm, Go's own doc generation). Separating template functions from template definitions allows both to be tested independently. The brief/paramDesc/returnDesc functions provide basic but correct descriptions — sophistication comes in Phase 5.

success_criteria: internal/template/engine.go compiles with go build ./internal/template/...
Engine.New accepts a map[string]string of template definitions and compiles them without error
Engine.Render with a valid symbol and language returns a non-empty string
Engine.Render with unknown language returns descriptive error
Engine.Render handles empty params gracefully (no panic, returns valid comment structure)
Engine.Render handles empty returns gracefully
Engine.Render handles nil receiver gracefully
All template functions (brief, paramDesc, returnDesc, joinParams, commentPrefix) produce sensible output for realistic inputs
go vet ./internal/template/ passes with zero warnings

tasks.title: Create internal/template/engine_test.go — Engine core tests
tasks.description: Write a comprehensive test suite for the template engine. Tests cover: template compilation from raw strings, Render output correctness for each supported language, edge cases (empty params, empty returns, nil receiver, generic type params), error handling (unknown language, template syntax errors), and helper function unit tests. Tests use simple inline template definitions to avoid coupling to the full template library — the template content tests belong in P2-T4.

test_cases.name: TestNewEngineCompiles
assertions: New with a valid template map returns Engine with no error
New with a syntactically invalid template string returns error
New with nil map returns error or empty engine (document behavior)

test_cases.name: TestRenderBasicFunction
test_cases.setup: Create Engine with a simple template: '{{.Name}} does things.', create SymbolInfo for a function 'DoSomething' with 2 params and 1 return
assertions: Render returns non-empty string
Output contains 'DoSomething' and 'does things'
Template variables are correctly interpolated

test_cases.name: TestRenderEmptyParams
test_cases.setup: Create SymbolInfo with Name='Foo', zero Params, zero Returns
assertions: Render does not panic
Output does not contain param-related text
Output does not contain return-related text

test_cases.name: TestRenderEmptyReturns
test_cases.setup: Create SymbolInfo with Name='Bar', 1 param, zero Returns
assertions: Render does not panic
Output contains param text
Output does not contain return text

test_cases.name: TestRenderWithReceiver
test_cases.setup: Create SymbolInfo with Kind=KindMethod, Receiver={Name:'p',Type:'*Person'}, Params=[{name,string}], Returns=['string']
assertions: Render does not panic
Output references receiver type or name in brief context
Receiver is not counted as a parameter

test_cases.name: TestRenderUnknownLanguage
test_cases.setup: Create Engine with only 'go' template, call Engine.Render with lang='python'
assertions: Render returns error containing 'unknown language' or 'python'

test_cases.name: TestBriefFunction
assertions: brief('Add', [{a,int},{b,int}], ['int']) returns a string containing 'adds' and 'a' and 'b'
brief('greet', [{name,str},{age,int}], ['str']) returns a string containing 'greets'
brief('DoNothing', [], []) returns a string that does not mention params or returns

test_cases.name: TestParamDescFunction
assertions: paramDesc([{a,int}], 0) returns a non-empty string
paramDesc([], 0) returns empty string (no params to describe)

test_cases.name: TestReturnDescFunction
assertions: returnDesc(['int']) returns a non-empty string
returnDesc([]) returns empty string

test_cases.name: TestJoinParams
assertions: joinParams([{a,int}]) returns 'a'
joinParams([{a,int},{b,int}]) returns 'a and b'
joinParams([{x,int},{y,int},{z,int}]) returns 'x, y, and z'

test_cases.name: TestRenderSpecialCharacters
test_cases.setup: Symbol name contains underscores or special characters (e.g., '__init__', 'process_data')
assertions: Template renders without escaping or truncation issues
Special characters in names are preserved in output

test_cases.name: TestRenderGenericTypeParams
test_cases.setup: SymbolInfo with TypeParams=[{T,any}], Name='FirstOrDefault'
assertions: Render does not panic with TypeParams present
Output references the generic type or handles gracefully

specification.test_data: Test data (SymbolInfo instances) are constructed inline in test functions, not loaded from fixture files. This keeps engine tests fast and independent of the parser package.
specification.rationale: Unit tests for the engine core should be independent of the template library (P2-T4 covers integration with real templates). Inline test data keeps tests fast (~100ms) and focused on engine behavior rather than data loading. Separate files for engine_test.go vs templates_test.go maintain clear separation of concerns.

success_criteria: All test cases pass with go test ./internal/template/ -v -count=1 -run TestEngine
Tests exercise all Render error paths (unknown language, template syntax error)
Tests cover empty params, empty returns, nil receiver, and generic type params
Helper function unit tests have >90% coverage of brief/paramDesc/returnDesc/joinParams
go vet ./internal/template/ passes with zero warnings
Tests complete in < 2 seconds (no CGo dependency, pure Go)

tasks.title: Create internal/template/templates.go — Per-language template definitions
tasks.description: Define the default Go text/template strings for each supported language: GoDoc prose, Doxygen for C/C++, Google-style Python docstrings, and Rustdoc. Each template uses the variable and function contract established by the engine (Name, Brief, Params, Returns, brief(), paramDesc(), returnDesc(), commentPrefix()). Templates are exported via GetDefaultTemplates() map[string]string, which the generator uses to initialize the engine. Also provides GetTemplate(lang string) (string, bool) for single-language lookup.

exports.func GetDefaultTemplates() map[string]st: Returns the default template map for all 5 languages. Keys: 'go', 'python', 'c', 'cpp', 'rust'. Each value is a Go text/template string.
exports.func GetTemplate(lang string) (string, b: Returns the default template for a single language. Returns empty string and false if language is unknown.

go.description: GoDoc prose-style comment. Uses '//' line prefix. Brief sentence followed by blank line then per-parameter descriptions.
format_notes: No @param or @return tags — Go convention is prose description
Blank comment line (//) separates brief from parameter descriptions
Every line starts with '// ' (Go comment prefix)
First word of Brief is lowercase (Go convention: '// FuncName does X')

python.description: Google-style Python docstring with Args: and Returns: sections. Uses triple-quoted """ delimiters.
format_notes: Args: section with 'name (type): Description' per param
python.example:
"""Greet a person.

Args:
    name (str): The name of the person.
    age (int): The age of the person.

Returns:
    str: The greeting message.
"""

c.description: Doxygen/JSDoc-style block comment for C. Uses /** ... */ with * prefix on each line.
c.example:
/**
 * @brief Divides dividend by divisor and returns the quotient.
 * @param dividend The dividend.
 * @param divisor The divisor.
 * @return int The quotient.
 */

cpp.description: Doxygen-style block comment for C++. Identical structure to C template — C and C++ share the Doxygen format.
format_notes: Same format as 'c' template — Doxygen conventions apply to both C and C++
May include class/struct brief descriptions in addition to function docs
Methods use 'this' or receiver context in brief descriptions
cpp.example:
/**
 * @brief Adds two integers and returns the sum.
 * @param a The first operand.
 * @param b The second operand.
 * @return int The sum.
 */

rust.description: Rustdoc-style with '///' prefix. Uses bullet-point parameter descriptions with backtick-quoted names.
format_notes: '/// Returns: Description' for return (or "# Returns" section header)

tag_order_configurability.description: Template tag order (brief first vs. param first) is configurable via the config system (P2-T5). The default templates above all use 'brief before parameters' order. The config file can select an alternate template with reversed order. Two template variants per language are predefined: 'default' (brief-first) and 'params-first'.
tag_order_configurability.alternative_variant: A 'params-first' variant reverses the order, placing @param/@arg lines before the brief/description. This is stored as a separate key (e.g., 'go-paramsfirst', 'c-paramsfirst') in the GetDefaultTemplates() map for config-based selection.

specification.rationale: Hardcoding the five template strings rather than reading them from config files at startup keeps the binary self-contained and reduces runtime failure modes. The config system can override individual template components (brief format, param format, return format) but the base structure comes from these defaults. C and C++ share the same Doxygen structure but are separate map entries to allow future divergence.

success_criteria: internal/template/templates.go compiles with go build ./internal/template/...
GetDefaultTemplates() returns a map with exactly 5 entries: 'go', 'python', 'c', 'cpp', 'rust'
Each template string contains valid Go text/template syntax (template.Must(template.New('test').Parse(tpl)) does not panic)
Each template references {{.Name}}, {{.Brief}}, {{range .Params}} or equivalent field accesses
C and C++ templates produce valid Doxygen /** */ block comment structures
Go template produces lines prefixed with '// ' (GoDoc convention)
Python template produces '"""' delimited docstrings with 'Args:' and 'Returns:' sections
Rust template produces lines prefixed with '///'
go vet ./internal/template/ passes with zero warnings

tasks.title: Create internal/template/templates_test.go — Golden file/snapshot tests per language
tasks.description: Write snapshot-style tests for each language's template output. Tests load SymbolInfo from the test fixtures (via the parser if available, or construct in-memory SymbolInfo), render through the Engine with default templates, and compare the output against golden files in testdata/golden/. Uses the -update flag pattern to regenerate golden files. Tests validate full comment structure including comment delimiters, indentation, and tag ordering.

inline_symbols.description: Test symbols constructed inline to avoid coupling tests to the parser at this stage. Each language has representative symbols matching the fixture patterns.

test_methodology.pattern: Golden file snapshot testing with -update flag
test_flow: For each language, load test symbols from inline data
Render each symbol through the engine for its language
Join multiple symbol outputs per language into a single expected block
If -update flag is passed and UPDATE_GOLDEN=true, write new golden files
test_methodology.update_flag: var update = flag.Bool('update', false, 'update golden files')

test_cases.name: TestGoDocGolden
assertions: Go Add function output starts with '// Add'
Go Add function output contains 'a -' and 'b -' param lines
Go output has exactly // prefix on every non-empty line
Golden file content matches rendered output

test_cases.name: TestPythonDocGolden
assertions: Python docstring starts with '"""' and ends with '"""'
Python docstring contains 'Args:' section
Python docstring contains 'Returns:' section when function has return type
Parameter lines are indented with 4 spaces
Golden file content matches rendered output

test_cases.name: TestCDoxygenGolden
assertions: C output starts with '/**' and ends with ' */'
C output contains '@brief' line
C output contains '@param' for each parameter
C output contains '@return' when return type is non-void
Golden file content matches rendered output

test_cases.name: TestCppDoxygenGolden
assertions: C++ output for functions matches same Doxygen conventions as C
C++ class output produces suitable class/structure comment
Golden file content matches rendered output

test_cases.name: TestRustdocGolden
assertions: Rust output starts with '///' prefix on every line
Rust output contains '* \`param\` -' bullet lines
Rust output contains 'Returns:' section when function has return type
Golden file content matches rendered output

test_cases.name: TestAllLanguagesCovered
assertions: Golden files exist for all 5 languages
Each golden file contains non-empty content
Number of golden files matches number of languages

golden_file_management.create_if_missing: If golden file does not exist and -update is not set, test fails with message showing expected content and instructions to run with -update
golden_file_management.overwrite: If -update is set, golden files are overwritten with current output
golden_file_management.diff_display: On mismatch, test displays unified diff between golden file and actual output
golden_file_management.cleanup: Test does not leave temporary files — output is compared in-memory against golden file content

specification.rationale: Golden file snapshot testing is the standard approach for template verification. The -update flag allows regenerating goldens when templates intentionally change. Testing with inline symbols (rather than loading from parser) isolates template correctness from parser correctness — a template test failure indicates a template problem, not a parser problem. Each language gets separate test functions for clear failure reporting.

success_criteria: All golden file tests pass with go test ./internal/template/ -v -count=1 -run TestTemplate
Golden files exist at testdata/golden/{go,python,c,cpp,rust}_expected.txt
Each golden file contains correctly formatted output for that language's convention
Tests pass both with existing goldens and after -update regeneration
Test output includes meaningful diff on mismatch (not just 'expected X got Y')
Go vet passes with zero warnings
Go template output from golden tests is syntactically valid: Go output parses as valid comment text, Python output parses as valid Python syntax

tasks.title: Create internal/config/config.go — Config file loader for .deoxy.yaml
tasks.description: Build the configuration system that loads a .deoxy.yaml file to customize doc comment generation per language. Supports: docstyle selection per language (GoDoc prose, Doxygen, Google-style, Rustdoc), tag ordering (brief-first vs params-first), custom tag injection, and override templates for brief/param/return components. Uses gopkg.in/yaml.v3 for YAML parsing. When no config file is found, uses sensible defaults matching the template library defaults. This is the first version of the config system — Phase 5 will add smart text toggles and custom tag registrations.

DocStyle.description: Enum for selecting the documentation comment style for a language.
values: DocStyleGoDoc = iota — Prose-style GoDoc (// FuncName does X)
DocStylePyDoc — Google-style Python docstrings ("""... Args: ... Returns: ...""")

TagOrder.description: Controls the order of sections in generated doc comments.
values: TagOrderBriefFirst = iota — Brief description before parameter/return tags (default)
TagOrderParamsFirst — Parameter tags before brief description

fields.name: DocStyle
fields.comment: Overrides the default docstyle for this language. Valid: 'godoc', 'doxygen', 'pydoc', 'rustdoc'

fields.name: TagOrder
fields.comment: Overrides tag order. Valid: 'brief-first', 'params-first'

fields.name: BriefTemplate
fields.comment: Custom Go text/template for the brief/description line. Overrides the default per-language brief template component.

fields.name: ParamTemplate
fields.comment: Custom template for individual parameter doc lines.

fields.name: ReturnTemplate
fields.comment: Custom template for return value doc lines.

fields.name: CustomTags
fields.comment: Additional tags to inject (e.g., '@note', '@example'). Injected after standard tags in declaration order.

fields.name: Version
fields.comment: Config file version for forward compatibility

fields.name: Languages
fields.comment: Per-language configuration overrides. Key is language name ('go', 'python', 'c', 'cpp', 'rust')

fields.name: DefaultDocStyle
fields.comment: Default docstyle for languages without explicit config. Valid: 'godoc', 'doxygen', 'pydoc', 'rustdoc'

fields.name: DefaultTagOrder
fields.comment: Default tag ordering: 'brief-first' or 'params-first'

exports.func LoadConfig(path string) (*Config, e: Loads and parses a .deoxy.yaml file from the given path. Returns Config and nil error on success. Returns error if file does not exist, YAML is invalid, or validation fails.
exports.func LoadDefaultConfig() *Config: Returns a Config with all default values — essentially what would be used if no .deoxy.yaml exists. DefaultDocStyle is 'godoc', DefaultTagOrder is 'brief-first'.
exports.func (c *Config) GetLanguageConfig(lang: Returns the effective config for a language, merging per-language overrides onto the defaults. Returns the default config if no per-language override exists.
exports.func (c *Config) GetDocStyle(lang string: Returns the parsed DocStyle enum for a language, considering defaults and per-language overrides.

yaml_schema.example:
version: '1'
default_style: godoc
default_tag_order: brief-first
languages:
  go:
    docstyle: godoc
    tag_order: brief-first
  c:
    docstyle: doxygen
    tag_order: params-first
  cpp:
    docstyle: doxygen
    custom_tags:
      - note
      - warning
  python:
    docstyle: pydoc
  rust:
    docstyle: rustdoc

validation_rules: DocStyle string values must be one of: 'godoc', 'doxygen', 'pydoc', 'rustdoc'
TagOrder string values must be one of: 'brief-first', 'params-first'
Unknown top-level keys in YAML produce a warning but do not cause a hard error (forward compatibility)
Unknown language keys in per-language config produce a warning (typo detection)
Version field is informational only; no version-specific behavior yet
specification.dependency: gopkg.in/yaml.v3 — a well-established Go YAML library (3.6k+ GitHub stars, 0 indirect deps). Added via 'go get gopkg.in/yaml.v3'. This is the only new external dependency for Phase 2 — the template engine itself uses only stdlib text/template.
specification.rationale: YAML is the standard for Go CLI tool config files (e.g., goreleaser, golangci-lint). yaml.v3 is the de facto standard Go YAML parser with zero dependencies. The Config struct is designed for forward compatibility: unknown YAML keys produce warnings instead of errors. The GetLanguageConfig() merge pattern allows minimal config files where only one or two languages need overrides. Per-language template overrides (BriefTemplate, ParamTemplate, ReturnTemplate) enable advanced users to customize output without recompiling.

success_criteria: internal/config/config.go compiles with go build ./internal/config/...
go get gopkg.in/yaml.v3 succeeds without dependency conflicts
LoadDefaultConfig() returns Config with Go defaulting to godoc style, C/C++ to doxygen, Python to pydoc, Rust to rustdoc
LoadConfig on a valid .deoxy.yaml returns correctly populated Config
LoadConfig on a non-existent file returns error
LoadConfig on an invalid YAML file returns error
GetLanguageConfig('go') returns merged config: default DocStyle=godoc unless overridden
GetDocStyle('c') returns DocStyleDoxygen from defaults
go vet ./internal/config/ passes with zero warnings
Multiple config fields round-trip correctly through YAML marshaling/unmarshaling

tasks.title: Create internal/config/config_test.go — Config loading and validation tests
tasks.description: Write the test suite for the config package. Tests cover: loading valid .deoxy.yaml files, missing file handling, YAML parse errors, per-language override merging, docstyle string-to-enum mapping, tag order parsing, unknown key warnings, and default config correctness. Uses temporary YAML files written in test setup for isolation.

test_cases.name: TestLoadValidConfig
test_cases.setup: Write a temporary .deoxy.yaml with go=godoc, c=doxygen, python=pydoc, rust=rustdoc
assertions: LoadConfig returns Config with no error
Config has correct docstyle for each language
Config fields match the YAML input

test_cases.name: TestLoadDefaultConfig
test_cases.setup: Call LoadDefaultConfig()
assertions: DefaultConfig is not nil
DefaultDocStyle is 'godoc'
DefaultTagOrder is 'brief-first'
All 5 languages have entries in Languages map

test_cases.name: TestLoadConfigFileNotFound
test_cases.setup: Call LoadConfig('/nonexistent/.deoxy.yaml')
assertions: Error is returned
Error message mentions file not found or no such file

test_cases.name: TestLoadConfigInvalidYAML
test_cases.setup: Write a temporary file with invalid YAML content
assertions: LoadConfig returns error
Error message mentions YAML parsing failure

test_cases.name: TestGetLanguageConfigMerge
test_cases.setup: Create Config with DefaultDocStyle='godoc', with per-language override for 'c' to 'doxygen'
assertions: GetLanguageConfig('go') returns DocStyle='godoc' (from defaults)
GetLanguageConfig('c') returns DocStyle='doxygen' (from override)
Go config does not inherit C's override

test_cases.name: TestGetDocStyleParsing
test_cases.setup: Create Config with various docstyle strings
assertions: GetDocStyle for 'godoc' returns DocStyleGoDoc
GetDocStyle for 'doxygen' returns DocStyleDoxygen
GetDocStyle for 'pydoc' returns DocStylePyDoc
GetDocStyle for 'rustdoc' returns DocStyleRustdoc
GetDocStyle for invalid string returns default with an error or warning

test_cases.name: TestUnknownKeyTolerance
test_cases.setup: Write a .deoxy.yaml with an unknown top-level key 'experimental_feature: true'
assertions: Config loads without error (forward compat)
Unknown key either is ignored or produces a non-fatal warning
Known fields are still populated correctly

test_cases.name: TestConfigRoundTrip
test_cases.setup: Create Config, marshal to YAML, unmarshal back
assertions: Round-tripped Config equals original
No data loss during marshal/unmarshal

test_cases.name: TestEmptyConfigFile
test_cases.setup: Write an empty .deoxy.yaml (just '{}')
assertions: Config loads without error
All values use defaults (DefaultDocStyle=godoc, etc.)

test_cases.name: TestPerLanguageTemplateOverrides
test_cases.setup: Write config with go.BriefTemplate='Custom: {{.Name}}', go.ParamTemplate='- {{.Name}}'
assertions: Config loads without error
Go LanguageConfig has BriefTemplate = 'Custom: {{.Name}}'
Go LanguageConfig has ParamTemplate = '- {{.Name}}'
Other languages have empty template overrides

test_cases.name: TestCustomTags
test_cases.setup: Write config with cpp.CustomTags=['note', 'warning']
assertions: Config loads without error
C++ LanguageConfig has CustomTags with ['note', 'warning']
Other languages have nil/empty CustomTags

specification.rationale: Config tests are pure Go + filesystem operations with no CGo dependency. Tests use temporary files in t.TempDir() for isolation and automatic cleanup. The round-trip test validates that marshaling and unmarshaling preserves all fields. The load-from-non-existent-file test validates graceful degradation.

success_criteria: All config tests pass with go test ./internal/config/ -v -count=1
Tests cover: valid config, invalid YAML, missing file, per-language overrides, defaults, custom tags, template overrides
Test coverage > 85% for config.go (statements)
Tests complete in < 1 second (no CGo, no network)
go vet passes with zero warnings

tasks.title: Create internal/generator/generator.go — Pipeline orchestrator (config → scan → parse → render)
tasks.description: Build the Generator — the top-level orchestrator that wires together the full deoxy pipeline: load config, scan directories for source files, parse files with tree-sitter, render doc comments via the template engine, and return the results. This package replaces the existing doc.go placeholder with a working Generator struct. The Generator does NOT write to files (Phase 3), but returns structured results that Phase 3's CLI commands can use for file injection. The pipeline is: Generator.Run(paths) → parse each file → group symbols by file → render comments → return map of file → GeneratedFileResult.

SymbolComment.description: A single generated doc comment for a symbol at a specific source location.

fields.name: Symbol
fields.comment: The parsed symbol this comment was generated for

fields.name: DocComment
fields.comment: The generated doc comment text, including comment delimiters

fields.name: StartLine
fields.comment: 0-indexed line where this comment should be inserted (before the symbol)

fields.name: EndLine
fields.comment: 0-indexed line where the symbol ends

GeneratedFileResult.description: Results for a single source file.

fields.name: Path
fields.comment: Absolute file path

fields.name: Language
fields.comment: Language identifier

fields.name: Source
fields.comment: Original source bytes (for Phase 3 injection)

fields.name: Comments
fields.comment: Generated comments per symbol, in source order

fields.name: ParseError
fields.comment: Non-nil if parsing failed for this file

fields.name: Files
fields.comment: Results per file

fields.name: FileCount
fields.comment: Total files processed

fields.name: SymbolCount
fields.comment: Total symbols documented

fields.name: ErrorCount
fields.comment: Files with errors

Generator.description: Top-level orchestrator for the deoxy pipeline.

fields.name: config
fields.comment: Configuration for docstyle, tag order, overrides

fields.name: parser
fields.comment: Shared tree-sitter parser manager (reused across files)

fields.name: engine
fields.comment: Template engine with compiled templates

methods: func New(cfg *config.Config) (*Generator, error) — creates Generator with config, initializes parser manager and template engine with default templates
func (g *Generator) Run(paths []string) (*GeneratorResult, error) — full pipeline: scan → parse → render. Returns structured results without modifying files.
func (g *Generator) Close() error — closes the parser manager, releasing CGo resources

step_1_scan.detail: Call scanner.Scan(paths...) to discover all supported source files
step_1_scan.error_handling: If scan returns error, wrap with context and return immediately

step_2_parse_render.detail: For each file group (by language), parse source through parser.Manager, run RunQuery to extract SymbolInfo, then render each symbol through template.Engine. Files can be processed in parallel since each file produces an independent Tree.
step_2_parse_render.error_handling: Per-file parse errors are captured in GeneratedFileResult.ParseError — they do not abort processing of other files. A file with only parse errors still appears in results with ParseError set.
step_2_parse_render.concurrency: Use a worker pool pattern (one per CPU) or process files sequentially with progress logging. Sequential is simpler and safer for initial implementation — parallel can be added in Phase 3.

step_3_collect.detail: Aggregate results into GeneratorResult with summary counts
step_3_collect.sorting: Files are sorted by path for deterministic output

config_integration.description: Generator uses the config to determine which template to use per language:
config_integration.mapping: Config docstyle → template selection: 'godoc' → go template, 'doxygen' → c/cpp template, 'pydoc' → python template, 'rustdoc' → rust template. Config TagOrder selects between 'brief-first' and 'params-first' template variants.
config_integration.override_templates: If LanguageConfig has BriefTemplate/ParamTemplate/ReturnTemplate set, the generator constructs a custom template string merging overrides with the default template structure.

specification.output_format: Generator.Run returns *GeneratorResult with all information needed by Phase 3's CLI commands (generate, diff, dry-run). File injection is NOT done here — the generator only produces in-memory results.
implementation_notes: Generator.New creates parser.Manager and closes it in Generator.Close (io.Closer pattern from Phase 1)
Generator.New creates template.Engine with GetDefaultTemplates() then applies config overrides
Each file's source bytes are read once and passed to both parser.Parse and stored in GeneratedFileResult.Source for Phase 3 writer
SymbolComment.StartLine uses SymbolInfo.StartLine (0-indexed from tree-sitter) — the writer in Phase 3 will insert the comment before this line
Panic recovery: Parse errors must not trigger panics — all errors are captured in result structs
specification.rationale: The generator is the central pipeline that Phase 3 CLI commands will call. It is intentionally a library component (not a CLI command) to maintain separation of concerns. The io.Closer interface on Generator ensures the parser pool is properly cleaned up. Returning structured results (rather than writing files) allows Phase 3 to implement diff/dry-run modes trivially.

success_criteria: internal/generator/generator.go compiles with go build ./internal/generator/...
Generator.New creates a generator with parser manager and template engine without error
Generator.Run on an existing source file returns non-nil result with at least 1 file and 1 symbol
Generator.Run on a non-existent directory returns error
Generator.Run processes files in all 5 languages when given the testdata/fixtures/ directory
Generator.Run error count equals the number of files that failed to parse
Each GeneratedFileResult has non-empty Comments for functions/methods that have params or returns
Generator.Close() releases all parser resources without error
Go vet passes with zero warnings
GeneratorResult.SymbolCount matches the expected count from known test fixtures

tasks.title: Create internal/generator/generator_test.go — End-to-end pipeline tests
tasks.description: Write integration tests for the full generator pipeline: scan real fixture files, parse through tree-sitter, render through templates, and verify generated doc comments meet expected format per language. These are true end-to-end tests that depend on CGo and tree-sitter grammars, unlike the isolated unit tests in P2-T2 and P2-T4. Each language gets a subtest that validates comment format, delimiters, parameter documentation, and return documentation.

test_cases.name: TestGenerateGoDoc
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config (go → godoc style), run on Go fixture files
assertions: At least 1 GeneratedFileResult with Language='go' and non-empty Comments
All comment lines for Go functions start with '// ' (Go comment prefix)
Parameter descriptions appear as '// paramName - description' pattern
Struct Person generates a struct-level comment (not param-based)
Method Greet generates a method-level comment mentioning receiver context
The Add function comment starts with '// Add' (function name + prose)

test_cases.name: TestGeneratePythonDoc
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config (python → pydoc style), run on Python fixture
assertions: At least 1 GeneratedFileResult with Language='python' and non-empty Comments
Docstrings are delimited by '"""' (opening and closing)
Arg descriptions follow 'name (type): Description' format
Returns section exists when function has return type annotation
Class Calculator comment describes the class (no param/return sections)

test_cases.name: TestGenerateCDoxygen
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config (c → doxygen style), run on C fixture
assertions: At least 1 GeneratedFileResult with Language='c' and non-empty Comments
Comment block starts with '/**' on its own line
Comment block ends with ' */' on its own line
Every intermediate line starts with ' * '
'@brief' tag is present
'@param' tags exist for each function parameter
'@return' tag exists for non-void functions
Void functions do NOT have @return tag

test_cases.name: TestGenerateCppDoxygen
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config (cpp → doxygen style), run on C++ fixture
assertions: At least 1 GeneratedFileResult with Language='cpp' and non-empty Comments
C++ function comments follow same Doxygen structure as C
Class Calculator gets a class-level comment

test_cases.name: TestGenerateRustdoc
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config (rust → rustdoc style), run on Rust fixture
assertions: At least 1 GeneratedFileResult with Language='rust' and non-empty Comments
All comment lines start with '/// ' (Rustdoc prefix)
Parameter bullets follow '* \`param\` - description' pattern
Return section exists for functions with return type
Struct Point gets a struct-level comment

test_cases.name: TestGenerateAllLanguages
test_cases.setup: Create Generator with default config, scan entire fixtures directory (all 5 languages)
assertions: Results contain files from all 5 languages
Total FileCount matches expected number of fixture files (at least 5)
Total SymbolCount > 0 and matches known minimum from fixtures
No file has ParseError set (all fixture files are valid syntax)

test_cases.name: TestGenerateWithConfigOverrides
test_cases.setup: Create a Config with Go set to Doxygen style (docstyle=doxygen), run on Go fixture
assertions: Go output now uses Doxygen /** */ format instead of // GoDoc format
Comment starts with '/**' and uses @brief, @param, @return
Parameter documentation uses @param tags
This validates the config → template mapping works end-to-end

test_cases.name: TestGenerateEmptyFileGraceful
test_cases.setup: Create a temporary empty .go file, run Generator on it
assertions: Result contains 1 file entry
Comments slice is empty (no symbols to document)
ParseError is nil

test_environment.cgo_required: These tests require CGO_ENABLED=1 and tree-sitter C libraries. They should be skipped if CGo is unavailable.
test_environment.fixture_dependency: Tests depend on testdata/fixtures/ from Phase 1 — if fixtures are missing, tests should skip with a clear message.
test_environment.timeout: Each language subtest should complete within 5 seconds. Full suite < 30 seconds.
test_environment.skip_short: These end-to-end tests can honor the -short flag — if -short is set, run only one language (Go) as a smoke test.

specification.rationale: End-to-end tests are the real gate for Phase 2 completion. They validate that the full pipeline works end-to-end with real source files and real tree-sitter parsing. The config override test (Go → Doxygen) validates that the config system effectively controls template selection. The skip_short pattern allows fast smoke tests during development while running the full suite before merge.

success_criteria: All generator tests pass with CGO_ENABLED=1 go test ./internal/generator/ -v -count=1
Go fixture: all function symbols generate GoDoc-style // comments with brief + param descriptions
Python fixture: greet function generates '"""... Args: ... Returns: ..."""' Google-style docstring
C fixture: functions generate '/** @brief ... @param ... @return ... */' Doxygen format
C++ fixture: functions generate Doxygen format, classes get class-level comments
Rust fixture: functions generate '///' comments with bullet-pointed params
Config override test proves Go can generate Doxygen-style comments when configured
All-languages test processes all 5 fixture directories without error
go vet passes with zero warnings
End-to-end test validates all 6 roadmap success criteria are met

execution_order.rationale: P2-T1 (engine.go) and P2-T5 (config.go) have zero file overlap and no code dependency between them — they target different packages (template vs config) with different imports. Engine uses stdlib text/template; Config introduces yaml.v3. Both can be built and tested in parallel. P2-T3 (templates.go) is intentionally assigned here despite being a 'template' file because it is pure constant definitions with zero imports (only text/template strings, no Go logic) — it can be created in parallel with engine.go since they share package but the template strings are compile-time constants that engine.go imports at runtime. However, to avoid confusion, placing P2-T3 in Wave 1 as well is fine since template strings are data not code. P2-T5 introduces the yaml.v3 dependency via 'go get' — this is the only new dependency for Phase 2.

P2-T3 (templates.go) defines the per-language template strings — must exist before engine_test.go can test with real templates. P2-T2 (engine_test.go) tests the engine but uses simple inline templates, so it does not strictly depend on P2-T3. However, assigning both to Wave 2 allows Wave 1 to focus on the two core implementations. P2-T3 is placed here rather than Wave 1 because the template strings reference the brief/paramDesc/returnDesc function contracts defined in P2-T1 — while they are just strings that compile independently, logically they depend on the function specification. P2-T2 and P2-T3 have no file overlap (engine_test.go vs templates.go) and can be created in parallel.

P2-T4 (templates_test.go) golden file tests depend on both engine (P2-T1) and templates (P2-T3) being available. P2-T6 (config_test.go) depends on config.go (P2-T5). These two test files target completely different packages (template vs config) and can be written in parallel.

P2-T7 (generator.go) depends on all four prior packages: config (P2-T5), template engine (P2-T1), template definitions (P2-T3), and the parser/scanner/symbol packages from Phase 1. P2-T8 (generator_test.go) depends on P2-T7. These must be the last tasks because the generator wires everything together. P2-T7 and P2-T8 share the generator package and should be done sequentially (P2-T7 first, then P2-T8), but they are grouped in the same wave for dependency ordering — within-wave sequencing handles the impl → test order.

verification.expected: exit code 0, new dependency added to go.mod
exit code 0, template package compiles with stdlib only
exit code 0, config package compiles with yaml.v3
exit code 0, generator package compiles with all dependencies
exit code 0, entire project compiles with CGO_ENABLED=1
exit code 0, zero vet warnings across all packages
exit code 0, all engine and template tests pass
exit code 0, all config tests pass
exit code 0, all generator tests pass (requires CGO_ENABLED=1)
exit code 0, full project test suite (short mode) passes

Golden file verification: Python greet function output
